gpu_inference_benchmark.name: LLM Inference Framework Benchmark
gpu_inference_benchmark.headline_anchor: Cross-GPU LLM inference benchmark showing SGLang achieving 5.4x throughput scaling A10G to H100 versus 2.5x for vLLM, across 300 ShareGPT prompts at concurrency 1-128

acronym_glossary.RadixAttention: keep (SGLang's KV cache prefix-reuse mechanism, NeurIPS 2024)

gpu_inference_benchmark.context: Independent benchmark extending DASH Lab at Northeastern's published vLLM vs Ollama A6000 comparison with two missing dimensions: SGLang and cross-GPU hardware scaling. Built a custom async benchmark harness using Python and async HTTP, ran Llama 3.1 8B AWQ-INT4 on AWS g5.xlarge (A10G, 24GB) and Vast.ai H100 (80GB SXM), sweeping concurrency 1/8/32/64/128 with 300 ShareGPT prompts per configuration, 10 warmup requests excluded. Published findings on Medium.
bullets: Built async benchmark harness comparing SGLang, vLLM, and Ollama on Llama 3.1 8B AWQ-INT4 across A10G and H100 GPUs, sweeping concurrency 1-128 with 300 ShareGPT prompts and measuring time-to-first-token, inter-token latency, and end-to-end P50/P95/P99 per configuration.

Found SGLang achieves 5.4x peak throughput scaling A10G to H100 (1,151 to 6,242 tokens/s) versus 2.5x for vLLM, tracing the gap to RadixAttention's KV cache prefix reuse exploiting H100's higher memory bandwidth, and single-user E2E P50 of 450ms on H100 versus 4,359ms for vLLM on the same hardware.

Diagnosed Ollama's concurrency failure as architectural rather than a configuration issue - llama.cpp's fixed-slot parallelism processes one request start-to-finish per slot with no iteration-level scheduling, causing success rate to drop from 100% at c=8 to 0.7% at c=128.

Extended DASH Lab's published vLLM vs Ollama A6000 benchmark with SGLang results and cross-GPU hardware-scaling analysis, published on Medium, and open-sourced the benchmark harness and visualizations on GitHub.

ncaa.name: NCAA March Madness Predictor
ncaa.headline_anchor: Top 20% finish (rank 599 of 3,000+) in Kaggle's NCAA prediction competition with a 29-feature XGBoost model blended against bookmaker odds

acronym_glossary.AUC: keep (recruiter parses as a score, technical reader knows it)

ncaa.context: Kaggle's March Machine Learning Mania 2026 - an annual prediction competition where 3,000+ data scientists submit win-probability models for every possible NCAA tournament matchup, scored on Brier loss against actual game outcomes. $10K first prize. Goal was to build a model that could compete with seasoned Kaggle grandmasters and beat published bookmaker odds.
bullets: Built end-to-end tournament prediction pipeline for Kaggle's March Machine Learning Mania 2026 (3,000+ competitors, $10K first prize), predicting win probabilities for all NCAA basketball matchups across men's and women's brackets, finishing top 20% (rank 599, Brier 0.1237 through 120 scored games).

Engineered 29-feature XGBoost model incorporating Elo ratings, generalized linear model team quality estimates (AUC 0.829 vs 0.811 seed-only baseline), and pace-adjusted box scores across a multi-season dataset, with disciplined ablation study rejecting 4 additional feature sets that each degraded performance.

Identified and fixed target leakage in spline calibration (pooled to per-fold fitting), reducing artificially optimistic cross-validation from 0.163 to honest 0.164, and implemented leave-one-season-out cross-validation excluding play-in games, matching 2025's gold medal Brier score (0.106) on historical backtest data.

Integrated bookmaker odds package providing direct pairwise probabilities for tournament matchups across all 68 teams per gender, eliminating a name-mapping pipeline with 59 missing matchups from fuzzy-matching collisions, and optimized blend weight (25% market + 75% ML) via backtesting against 2024-2025 actual outcomes.

Diagnosed and resolved data quality issues including a column swap affecting 2021-2026 seasons (confirmed via possession estimate divergence analysis), and migrated from manual Excel-based team name mapping to API-based integration reducing blend code from ~100 lines to 5 with zero mapping errors.

reddit_pipeline.name: Reddit Data Pipeline - ETL System
reddit_pipeline.headline_anchor: ETL pipeline ingesting 700+ daily posts across 14 subreddits with anomaly detection surfacing emerging product issues earlier than support-ticket review

acronym_glossary.TF-IDF: keep (technical reader knows; recruiter parses as 'text clustering method')

reddit_pipeline.context: Originally built as a take-home for a Tesla data engineering interview. Problem: product teams typically learn about emerging issues from support ticket aggregations that lag actual customer frustration by days. Reddit discussions surface the same issues hours after they appear. Goal was to ingest active product-discussion subreddits, run sentiment and anomaly detection on the stream, and surface emerging issues to a dashboard PMs could check daily.
bullets: Built end-to-end pipeline pulling 700+ daily posts across 14 product-discussion subreddits to surface emerging issues earlier than support-ticket aggregation, using Kafka ingestion, Apache Airflow (6 scheduled jobs) for orchestration, and PostgreSQL for storage.

Implemented multi-method anomaly detection using z-score analysis, volume spike detection, and TF-IDF K-Means clustering, surfacing issues at a 0.16% alert rate from thousands of posts with zero false positives on high-severity incidents.

Built Next.js dashboard with 5 interactive views (sentiment trends, product-level breakdowns, real-time alerts) so product managers could investigate spikes the same day they appeared, replacing weekly manual review of subreddit threads.

credit_risk.name: Credit Risk Analysis Dashboard
credit_risk.headline_anchor: Tableau dashboard surfacing borrower-level default risk across 300,000+ loan applications, with a Python-engineered risk scoring engine validated through interactive visualizations

acronym_glossary.LOD: Level of Detail expression - Tableau's mechanism for computing aggregations independent of dashboard filters

credit_risk.context: End-to-end project using the Home Credit Default Risk dataset - 307,511 real anonymized loan applications. Built a Python pipeline to clean, engineer features, and score borrower risk, then ported the full analysis to an interactive Tableau Public dashboard. Goal was to surface portfolio-level risk patterns that would otherwise require hours of application-by-application Excel review.
bullets: Built end-to-end Python pipeline on 300,000+ loan applications - handled missingness across 67 columns with strategy-specific imputation, engineered a custom risk score from education, income type, and application history, and segmented borrowers into Low, Medium, and High risk tiers using quantile-based bucketing.

Developed interactive Tableau dashboard with 6 performance metrics, dual-axis age trend chart, dynamic Top N occupation filter, donut chart for risk distribution, and Level of Detail expressions ensuring occupation default rates remain population-stable when dashboard filters are applied.

Surfaced that low-skill laborers default at 17.5% - more than double the 8.2% baseline - and previously rejected applicants default at 14.4% vs 7.5% for approved ones, giving underwriting teams a tier-based risk view without re-running queries.

dermrx_agent.name: DermRx Agent
dermrx_agent.headline_anchor: Agentic clinical pipeline compressing cross-specialty drug interaction checks from 5-10 minute manual lookups to under 60 seconds

acronym_glossary.RAG: keep if JD uses RAG; otherwise 'retrieval-augmented generation'
acronym_glossary.CYP2C9: keep in pharmacology bullets; drop in non-pharma framing
acronym_glossary.CYP3A4: keep in pharmacology bullets; drop in non-pharma framing

dermrx_agent.context: Solo submission to Google's MedGemma Impact Challenge 2026 - a competition for novel applications of Google's medical foundation models (MedGemma, MedSigLIP, TxGemma). Real clinical problem: a dermatologist seeing a patient on warfarin needs to check whether prescribing fluconazole for fungal infection will cause a major drug interaction. Currently this requires manual lookup chains across DDInter, drug references, and prescriber notes - 5-10 minutes per check. Goal was to compress this to under 60 seconds via an agentic pipeline combining visual diagnosis, treatment selection, and safety screening.
bullets: Built agentic clinical pipeline integrating MedSigLIP, MedGemma 4B, and TxGemma 2B that compresses cross-specialty drug interaction checks from 5-10 minute manual lookup chains to under 60 seconds, covering a database of 100,000+ drug-drug interactions.

Designed three-tier classification framework consolidating 141 dermatological conditions into 22 clinically actionable categories with 76 optimized text prompts, improving MedSigLIP zero-shot top-1 accuracy from 13% to 71% for treatable conditions while maintaining 100% melanoma detection safety.

Identified critical model limitation where TxGemma 2B misclassified fluconazole as a non-CYP2C9 inhibitor (traced to training data exclusions), redesigned architecture into a complementary multi-model system where DDInter handles drug-drug interactions and TxGemma handles molecular toxicity analysis.

Built evidence-based treatment formulary of 170 FDA-sourced drugs by querying the MED-RT drug-class API across 11 therapeutic classes, cross-referencing each drug against DDInter for safety coverage and PubChem for molecular structure representations.

Developed FastAPI backend with 7 REST endpoints including drug autocomplete search across 1,867 drugs, pairwise interaction checking by ID or name, automatic batching for patients on 6+ medications, and health monitoring - with full-stack Next.js 15 frontend showing visible reasoning trace at each pipeline step.

Built 3 Kaggle research notebooks documenting MedSigLIP classification experiments, DDInter 2.0 integration with 100,000+ curated drug-drug interactions across 1,867 drugs, and TxGemma toxicity prediction validation - all running within a 16GB GPU memory budget.

cmi_bfrb.name: CMI - Detect Behavior with Sensor Data
cmi_bfrb.headline_anchor: Subject-independent gesture classifier achieving 0.7868 macro+binary F1 across 81 subjects and 8,151 sequences from a wrist-worn sensor device
cmi_bfrb.context: Kaggle competition hosted by the Child Mind Institute for detecting repetitive hand behaviors (hair-pulling, skin-picking, nail-biting) from a wrist-worn sensor device. Real clinical motivation: BFRBs are common in trichotillomania and OCD patients, and continuous passive detection from a wearable could enable just-in-time intervention. Dataset is 8,151 labeled gesture sequences from 81 subjects. Competition goal was to classify 18 hand gestures with subject-independent generalization.
bullets: Built subject-grouped 5-fold cross-validation pipeline ensuring zero subject overlap between train and validation sets, preventing same-person data leakage that inflates scores by 0.05+ in naive splits, as the foundation for honest model comparison across 8,151 gesture sequences from 81 subjects.

Engineered 16-channel feature set from 7 raw accelerometer and gyroscope columns: gravity-removed linear acceleration via quaternion-rotated vector subtraction, jerk (first derivative), per-axis magnitudes, and angular velocity from consecutive quaternion deltas - single change improved fold-0 score from 0.6872 to 0.7522 (+0.065) with no architectural modifications.

Designed augmentation pipeline (time stretch +/-20%, time shift +/-15%, Gaussian noise, mixup with KL-divergence loss on soft labels) that pushed best training epoch from 22 to 39+ across folds, eliminating early overfitting and improving fold-0 score by an additional +0.033 to 0.7853.

Architected 1D convolutional network with attention blocks and bidirectional recurrent network (3 residual SE blocks - 2-layer biGRU - mean pooling - neural network head) achieving 0.7868 macro+binary F1 on subject-grouped 5-fold cross-validation, completing full training in ~10 minutes on a single GPU.

rag_qa_system.name: RAG Q&A System
rag_qa_system.headline_anchor: Production retrieval-augmented Q&A pipeline with automated evaluation, full-chain observability, and CI/CD to AWS

acronym_glossary.RAG: keep if JD uses RAG; otherwise 'retrieval-augmented generation'

rag_qa_system.context: Personal portfolio project building a production-grade retrieval-augmented Q&A system. Goal was to go beyond a notebook-grade RAG demo and ship something with real evaluation (automated framework measuring faithfulness and answer relevancy on every query), real observability (LangSmith tracing for token usage and latency per query), and a real automated deployment pipeline (GitHub Actions to AWS App Runner with security scanning).
bullets: Engineered production retrieval-augmented Q&A pipeline using LangChain, OpenAI embeddings, and Qdrant Cloud with cosine similarity search, serving 8 async FastAPI endpoints including streaming responses and multi-format document ingestion (PDF, TXT, CSV) with recursive chunking.

Integrated automated evaluation framework measuring answer faithfulness and answer relevancy on each query, achieving 0.88 average answer relevancy across test queries, with async execution and graceful degradation on edge cases.

Built 33-test pytest suite with async mock fixtures for OpenAI, Qdrant, and evaluation dependencies, covering query validation, scoring, source attribution, streaming, and error handling, enforced via GitHub Actions CI with Ruff and Black linting.

Containerized with multi-stage Docker build (Python 3.12-slim builder to non-root production image with health checks) and automated deployment pipeline to AWS App Runner via container registry with zero-downtime updates and Bandit security scanning.

Added LangSmith tracing for full retrieval chain observability (token usage, latency, cost per query) so retrieval failures could be traced to the specific chunk-retrieval step, plus liveness and readiness health endpoints with vector database connectivity verification.
